prompts.text: Explain why a local language model may stream expert weights from SSD instead of keeping every parameter in memory. Cover the main latency, memory, and reliability tradeoffs, then give one practical recommendation for a sixteen gigabyte workstation.

You are reviewing a CPU-only inference runtime for a very large mixture-of-experts language model on a workstation with sixteen gigabytes of memory and a fast NVMe SSD. Write a concise technical assessment. Explain how a small resident dense trunk, an expert cache, compressed key-value state, and disk streaming interact. Discuss what changes between a cold run and a warm run, why page cache pressure can improve or destroy throughput, and why swap activity must be treated as a correctness signal rather than hidden. Compare buffered reads with direct I/O, explain how top-p expert routing may reduce disk work, and describe the possible benefit and cost of a multi-token prediction head. State which measurements are needed to make the result reproducible: prompt token count, prefill and decode throughput, peak resident memory, expert-cache hit rate, bytes read from disk per generated token, swap deltas, storage temperature, software revision, model identity, and exact environment variables. End with a clear rule for deciding whether the model runs normally, runs in a degraded state, or does not run on this host. Do not invent benchmark values.

Prepare an engineering review of a baseline experiment for running a very large mixture-of-experts language model locally on a CPU-only machine with sixteen gigabytes of memory. The checkpoint is stored on a fast solid-state drive and most expert weights cannot remain resident. Begin by describing the experimental question and why a real baseline must precede optimization. Explain the expected memory layout: resident embeddings and dense attention weights, compressed key-value state, temporary working buffers, a small per-layer cache of routed experts, and operating-system page cache. Describe how the runtime chooses a memory budget from available memory and why a hard resident-set ceiling must reject a configuration whose dense trunk and minimum expert cache cannot fit. Then define a reproducible cold-run procedure. It must identify the host, source revision, compiler, operating system, filesystem, storage device, checkpoint files, configuration hashes, and exact command. Explain how the operator should establish and attest a cold cache without allowing the capture script to make privileged system changes automatically. Define three warm repetitions and explain what may improve as the operating-system page cache and learned expert-usage data become warm. Discuss measurement of prompt size, time to first generated token, prefill throughput, decode throughput, peak resident set, process disk bytes read, bytes read per generated token, expert-cache hits and misses, process swap, host swap deltas, and storage temperature. State that unavailable metrics must be null with a machine-readable reason and must never be estimated. Next define controlled comparisons. Hold prompt, generated-token limit, source revision, checkpoint, and host constant while comparing default expert routing against top-p zero point seven, multi-token prediction disabled against enabled, and buffered I/O against direct I/O. Explain why each comparison needs repeated observations and why a failed doctor check, hard memory gate, out-of-memory termination, or swap storm is still a useful result. Describe how raw standard output, standard error, commands, environment overrides, timestamps, return codes, and cryptographic hashes make the receipt auditable. Include safeguards: refuse hosts outside the declared memory tolerance, refuse tiny fixtures presented as the production checkpoint, refuse missing engine binaries, preserve failed executions, avoid downloading weights, avoid deleting caches, avoid privileged commands, and never convert a partial matrix into a successful verdict. Finish with explicit verdict rules. Runs normally means every required case completed without a memory-gate failure, out-of-memory event, or swap storm. Runs degraded means every required case produced output but one or more declared degradation thresholds were crossed. Does not run means planning rejected the minimum budget, the engine was killed for memory, or required cases could not generate output. Incomplete means evidence is missing or a required metric remains unobservable. List the remaining follow-up work that optimization milestones should use from this baseline, especially the measured resident-memory gap, disk traffic per token, cache hit rate, and the most effective controlled variation. Do not provide or infer numerical results.
